key_insight: The paper proposes using embedding-based Mahalanobis distance to quantify how non-trivial machine-generated mathematical conjectures are, helping assess whether they meet a Birch-test-style standard of genuine novelty.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical evaluation layer for automated theorem-proving and conjecture-mining systems, especially when human review is the bottleneck for judging novelty and correctness.
method: Run HypothesiX to mine conjectures, with a focus on twin-prime-related statements.
Embed machine-generated conjectures alongside known conjectures and measure cluster distance via Mahalanobis distance.
Use the resulting score as a non-triviality benchmark and an error-indication signal for localization of likely incorrect statements.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports an analysis of HypothesiX-generated conjectures and proposes the benchmark as a way to evaluate them against selected known mathematical conjectures in embedding space.
watch_item: The benchmark still depends on the quality and representativeness of the chosen conjecture embedding cluster, so biased reference sets could distort the non-triviality score.
paper_title: Mapping Mathematical Hardness: Machine-Assisted Conjecture Discovery and the Quantification of Non-Triviality
